Born in Vienna to one of Europe\

nodes.name: Platon
nodes.description: Gruendet die Akademie und entwickelt Ideenlehre, Paideia und politische Philosophie.

nodes.name: Sokrates
nodes.description: Philosophiert ueber Pruefung, Gewissen und dialogische Selbsterkenntnis.

nodes.name: Pythagoras
nodes.description:
Pythagoras of Samos (c. 570–495 BCE) is one of the most mysterious and influential figures in the history of Western thought — a philosopher, mathematician, and religious leader whose community at Croton (Southern Italy) combined rigorous mathematical investigation with mystical practice and a strict way of life (vegetarianism, silence, communal property, reincarnation belief). He wrote nothing, and all accounts of his teaching are mediated through later sources, yet his influence on Plato, Neoplatonism, and the Western intellectual tradition is incalculable. The Pythagorean theorem, though not discovered by him (it was known in Babylonian and Egyptian mathematics earlier), represents the tradition that bears his name and the insight that mathematical relations govern physical reality.

Pythagoras\

Verbindet Zahl, Kosmos und Lebensform in einer philosophischen Gemeinschaft.

nodes.name: Baruch de Spinoza
nodes.description: Baruch (Benedictus de) Spinoza (1632–1677) is the most systematically rigorous and philosophically radical thinker of the 17th century — excommunicated from the Amsterdam Jewish community at age 23 for reasons still debated by scholars, he spent his life as a lens-grinder, in correspondence with leading European intellectuals, and composing philosophical works of extraordinary depth and compression. His Ethics (Ethica Ordine Geometrico Demonstrata, published posthumously in 1677) is written in the deductive form of Euclidean geometry: definitions, axioms, propositions, demonstrations, and corollaries — as if the nature of God, mind, and human freedom could be logically derived from self-evident starting points. This formal audacity reflects Spinoza\

nodes.name: Immanuel Kant
nodes.description: Untersucht die Bedingungen von Erkenntnis, Freiheit und Urteilskraft.

nodes.name: René Descartes
nodes.description: Begruendet den methodischen Zweifel und verschiebt Gewissheit in das denkende Ich.

nodes.name: Jean-Paul Sartre
nodes.description: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) is the defining figure of French existentialism and one of the last great European public intellectuals, whose work simultaneously transformed academic philosophy, literature, political theory, and cultural life. His 1000-page Being and Nothingness (1943), written partly while a prisoner of war and in German-occupied Paris, is the comprehensive statement of his existentialist ontology. The fundamental ontological distinction is between being-in-itself (être-en-soi) — the dense, self-identical being of objects, which simply \

nodes.name: Albert Camus
nodes.description: Verbindet Sinnkrise, Revolte und Haltung ohne metaphysische Absicherung.

nodes.name: Søren Kierkegaard
nodes.description: Søren Kierkegaard (1813–1855) is the founding figure of existentialism and one of the most original and psychologically penetrating thinkers in the history of philosophy. Born in Copenhagen to a wealthy, deeply religious father whose personal guilt and spiritual crisis profoundly marked his son, Kierkegaard lived his entire short life (42 years) in Denmark, producing an enormous output of philosophical, theological, and psychological works under various pseudonyms as well as under his own name. His broken engagement to Regine Olsen — which he felt incapable of maintaining, perhaps due to the burden of his father\

nodes.name: Porphyrios
nodes.description: Schueler Plotins und Herausgeber der Enneaden. Vermittelt Neuplatonismus in systematischer Form.

nodes.name: Proklos
nodes.description: Verfeinert die gestufte Ontologie des Neuplatonismus und die Vermittlungslogik zwischen Ebenen.

nodes.name: Plotin
nodes.description: Entfaltet das Eine, den Nous und die Rueckkehr der Seele als gestufte Ontologie.

nodes.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
nodes.description:
Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) is the most explosive and influential philosopher of the 19th century, whose radical critique of European morality, religion, and metaphysics fundamentally shaped the 20th century in philosophy, literature, psychology, and politics. Born in Röcken, Saxony, to a Lutheran pastor\

Schneidet die Herkunft moralischer Werte auf und verschiebt Denken in Kritik und Umwertung.

nodes.name: Bodhidharma
nodes.description: Bodhidharma (c. 5th–6th century CE) is the semi-legendary figure credited with transmitting Chan (Zen) Buddhism from India to China and is venerated as the First Patriarch of Chan. Historical sources are sparse and conflicting; the most credible early biography dates him to arriving in China around 520 CE, during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang. The famous anecdote of Emperor Wu asking about the merit of his Buddhist activities — \

nodes.name: Dōgen Zenji
nodes.description: Dogen Zenji (1200–1253) is the founder of the Soto school of Zen in Japan and the most philosophically original religious thinker in Japanese history. Born into an aristocratic family, orphaned young, and deeply moved by the Buddhist question of why we must practice if Buddha-nature is already complete, he traveled to China in 1223 to study with the Chan master Rujing, under whom he achieved profound awakening through the instruction \

nodes.name: Nagarjuna
nodes.description:
Nagarjuna (c. 150–250 CE) is the most important Buddhist philosopher after the Buddha himself, founder of the Madhyamaka (Middle Way) school and the systematic expositor of sunyata (emptiness) as the central insight of the Mahayana. His principal work, the Mulamadhyamakakarika (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way), systematically demonstrates through rigorous logical analysis that no dharma (phenomenon) — whether matter, mind, causality, time, space, or even the Buddha and Nirvana — possesses svabhava (inherent existence, own-being). Everything arises dependently (pratityasamutpada), which means everything is empty of intrinsic nature. This is not nihilism: Nagarjuna famously argues that emptiness itself is empty — to reify sunyata into a positive metaphysical claim is to misunderstand it.

Nagarjuna employed the technique of prasanga (reductio ad absurdum) with extraordinary sophistication, showing that any philosophical position that asserts inherent existence leads to internal contradictions. His targets included not only Hindu Nyaya-Vaisheshika metaphysics but also Abhidharma Buddhist positions that had reified dharmas into ultimate building-blocks of reality. The revolutionary move is the equation of sunyata with pratityasamutpada with the Middle Way itself: to say that things are empty is to say they arise dependently, which is to say they have conventional but not ultimate reality. This allows Nagarjuna to maintain the conventional validity of Buddhist ethics, practice, and the path while insisting that no entity, including the practitioner, the path, or liberation, has ultimate ontological standing.

Madhyamaka spread throughout Asia in two major branches: Prasangika (Buddhapalita, Chandrakirti), which pushed the reductio method to its logical limits without any positive counter-thesis; and Svatantrika (Bhavaviveka, Kamalashila), which employed independent syllogistic reasoning alongside the reductio. In Tibet, the debate between these schools became central to the Gelug curriculum, with Tsongkhapa arguing that Chandrakirti\

Begruendet die Madhyamaka-Schule und entwickelt die Philosophie der Leerheit.

nodes.name: Patanjali
nodes.description:
Patanjali is the author of the Yoga Sutras, the most authoritative and systematic text on classical yoga, traditionally dated to between 400 BCE and 400 CE (most scholars favor c. 400 CE). Virtually nothing is known of him historically; tradition conflates him with the grammarian Patanjali who wrote the Mahabhashya commentary on Panini\

Verfasser der Yoga Sutras. Systematisiert den achtgliedrigen Pfad des Yoga.

nodes.name: Sri Ramana Maharshi
nodes.description:
Ramana Maharshi (1879–1950) is one of the most influential Hindu spiritual figures of the 20th century and a towering representative of the Advaita Vedanta tradition — remarkable for the combination of intellectual simplicity, radical directness, and profound inner authority of his teaching. Born Venkataraman Iyer in Tiruchuzhi, Tamil Nadu, he underwent a spontaneous and complete spiritual transformation at age sixteen without any teacher, scripture, or formal practice: overcome by what felt like an approaching death, he lay down, held himself completely rigid, imagined himself dead, and discovered with absolute certainty that the \

Lehrt Selbsterforschung durch die Frage "Wer bin ich?" ohne Ritual und Systemzwang.

nodes.name: Thich Nhat Hanh
nodes.description:
Thich Nhat Hanh (1926–2022) is the most influential Buddhist teacher of the 20th century after the Dalai Lama, the founder of \

Begruendet einen engagierten Buddhismus und popularisiert Achtsamkeit als Lebenskunst.

nodes.name: Martin Heidegger
nodes.description: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) is the most influential and most controversial philosopher of the 20th century, whose Being and Time (1927) fundamentally transformed Western philosophy by reorienting the \

nodes.description: Lehrt innere Freiheit durch die Unterscheidung dessen, was in unserer Macht steht und was nicht.

nodes.name: Seneca
nodes.description:
Lucius Annaeus Seneca (c. 4 BCE – 65 CE) is the most literary and psychologically sophisticated of the Roman Stoics, a paradoxical figure who combined the most demanding philosophical ideals with a life of enormous wealth, political intrigue, and moral compromise. Born in Corduba (Roman Spain) to a prosperous family, he became Rome\

Verbindet stoische Lebenskunst mit politischer Verantwortung und Selbstpruefung.

nodes.name: Alan Watts
nodes.description:
Alan Watts (1915–1973) is the most influential interpreter of Asian philosophy to Western audiences in the 20th century, a brilliantly gifted communicator whose books, lectures, and broadcasts introduced millions of Americans and Europeans to Zen Buddhism, Daoism, Hinduism, and Vedanta at a time when these traditions were almost unknown in the West. Born in Chislehurst, England, he developed a passion for Oriental philosophy as a teenager through the Buddhist Lodge in London, moved to the United States in 1938, was ordained as an Episcopal priest (1945–1950) before leaving the ministry, and eventually settled in the San Francisco Bay Area where he became a central figure in the countercultural spiritual milieu of the 1950s–70s. His Friday evening seminars at the American Academy of Asian Studies, his radio broadcasts on KPFA, and later his recorded talks (thousands of hours of which are freely available online) reached audiences of extraordinary breadth.

Watts\

nodes.name: Martin Buber
nodes.description: Martin Buber (1878–1965) is one of the most important Jewish thinkers of the 20th century, best known for his 1923 philosophical masterpiece I and Thou (Ich und Du), which introduced a fundamental distinction that has influenced philosophy, theology, psychology, and education worldwide. Born in Vienna and raised partly by his grandfather Solomon Buber (a renowned Midrash scholar) in Galicia after his parents\

nodes.name: Meister Eckhart
nodes.description:
Meister Eckhart (c. 1260–1328) is the most philosophically radical and intellectually demanding of the Christian mystics, a Dominican friar whose Latin scholastic works and German vernacular sermons pushed the boundaries of orthodox Christian thought with an apophatic and non-dualist boldness that earned posthumous papal condemnation. Born in Hochheim (Thuringia), he held the prestigious Chair of Theology at the University of Paris (twice, an unusual distinction) and served as prior provincial of Saxony and vicar general of Bohemia. His Latin works — the Opus Tripartitum — deploy the full apparatus of scholastic philosophy in service of mystical insight; his German sermons translate this into a vernacular register that shaped the development of philosophical German prose.

Eckhart\

Lehrt Gelassenheit als Loslassen aller Bilder, selbst der Bilder von Gott.

nodes.name: Parmenides
nodes.description: Stellt dem Wandel die These eines unveraenderlichen Seins entgegen.

nodes.name: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
nodes.description: Lucius Annaeus Seneca (c. 4 BCE – 65 CE) is the most literary and psychologically sophisticated of the Roman Stoics, a paradoxical figure who combined the most demanding philosophical ideals with a life of enormous wealth, political intrigue, and moral compromise. Born in Corduba (Roman Spain) to a prosperous family, he became Rome\

nodes.name: Simone Weil
nodes.description:
Simone Weil (1909–1943) is one of the most singular spiritual and philosophical figures of the 20th century — a French philosopher, mystic, political activist, and saint manquée who died of self-imposed starvation (refusing to eat more than the wartime ration of occupied France) in a sanatorium in Ashford, England, at age 34. Brilliant, uncompromising, and physically fragile, she graduated first in her class at the École Normale Supérieure (ahead of Simone de Beauvoir), worked in factories to understand the condition of the working class from the inside, fought briefly in the Spanish Civil War, and underwent a series of mystical experiences that transformed her philosophical vision without leading her to formal conversion to Christianity — she declined baptism throughout her life, refusing to enter the Church while millions outside it suffered.

Weil\

nodes.name: Stoizismus – Die Kunst des guten Lebens
Zhuangzi
nodes.description:
Zhuangzi (Zhuang Zhou, c. 369–286 BCE) is the second great Daoist sage and, in the judgment of many scholars, one of the greatest philosophers and literary artists in world history. A contemporary of Mencius, he reportedly served briefly as a minor official before withdrawing from public life. The text bearing his name (the Zhuangzi, or Nanhua jing) is traditionally divided into Inner Chapters (attributed to Zhuangzi himself), Outer Chapters, and Miscellaneous Chapters (later compilations). The Inner Chapters — seven essays of extraordinary philosophical depth and literary inventiveness — are among the most remarkable texts in any tradition.

Where Laozi\

Meister der Paradoxie und der Relativierung aller Standpunkte.

nodes.name: Peter L. Berger und Thomas Luckmann
Ram Dass
nodes.description: Ram Dass (born Richard Alpert, 1931–2019) is one of the most influential figures in the transmission of Eastern spirituality to the Western world, whose life trajectory — Harvard psychology professor, colleague of Timothy Leary in psychedelic research, spiritual seeker in India, and eventually teacher and author — traced the arc of the American spiritual counterculture from the 1960s to the 21st century. Born in Boston to a wealthy Jewish family, he received his PhD in psychology from Stanford and was appointed to Harvard\

nodes.name: Das holografische Prinzip und die Stringtheorie
Milarepa
nodes.description: Milarepa (c. 1052–1135) is the defining saint and most beloved figure of Tibetan Buddhism, whose extraordinary life story — from black magician who killed his enemies to one of the greatest yogis Tibet has produced — embodies the tradition\

nodes.name: Dogen Zenji
nodes.description: Dogen Zenji (1200–1253) is the founder of the Soto school of Zen in Japan and the most philosophically original religious thinker in Japanese history. Born into an aristocratic family, orphaned young, and deeply moved by the Buddhist question of why we must practice if Buddha-nature is already complete, he traveled to China in 1223 to study with the Chan master Rujing, under whom he achieved profound awakening through the instruction \

nodes.name: Hui Neng
nodes.description: Hui Neng (638–713 CE) is the Sixth Patriarch of Chan Buddhism and the figure whose teachings established the Southern School of sudden awakening that became the fountainhead of virtually all subsequent Zen Buddhism in China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. His story — an illiterate woodcutter who achieved enlightenment upon hearing a single verse of the Diamond Sutra — became the paradigmatic Chan story of immediate, unconditional awakening: Buddha-nature is not achieved through study, intellectual cultivation, or gradual practice but is recognized directly and completely in a single moment of clear seeing. The contest between his enlightenment verse (\

nodes.name: Hildegard von Bingen
nodes.description:
Hildegard of Bingen (1098–1179) is one of the most extraordinary figures of the medieval period: Benedictine abbess, visionary mystic, composer, natural philosopher, physician, poet, and prophet. She experienced visions from childhood but kept them secret until age 42, when a divine command (and the encouragement of her confessor) led her to write them down in the Scivias (Know the Ways), her first major visionary work. Hildegard described her visions as the \

Verbindet Mystik, Musik, Naturbeobachtung und kosmische Bildwelten.

nodes.name: Teresa von Ávila
nodes.description: Teresa of Ávila (1515–1582) is the most practically precise and psychologically sophisticated guide to Christian contemplative prayer in the Western tradition — a Doctor of the Church (so designated in 1970, one of the first women to receive this title), a reformer of the Carmelite order, and a mystic who combined extraordinary spiritual depth with formidable organizational and literary gifts. Born Teresa Sánchez de Cepeda y Ahumada in Ávila, Spain, to a converso (Jewish-convert) family, she entered the Carmelite convent of the Incarnation at age 20 but spent twenty years in spiritual mediocrity — distracted by social life in the parlor, neglecting contemplative prayer — before her \

nodes.name: Adi Shankara
nodes.description:
Adi Shankara (c. 788–820 CE) is the most influential theologian and philosopher in the history of Hinduism, whose systematic elaboration of Advaita Vedanta shaped the entire subsequent development of Indian philosophical discourse. Born in Kalady, Kerala, he was, according to tradition, a child prodigy who renounced the world at age eight and sought out his teacher Govindapada. Within a remarkably short life — tradition holds he died at 32 — he composed commentaries on the ten principal Upanishads, the Brahma Sutras, and the Bhagavad Gita, as well as independent works such as the Vivekachudamani (Crest Jewel of Discrimination) and Upadesa Sahasri (Thousand Teachings). He also founded four monastic centers (mathas) at the four cardinal points of India — Sringeri, Dwarka, Puri, and Badrinath — which continue to function today.

Shankara\

Begruender des Advaita Vedanta. Lehrt Nicht-Zweiheit von Atman und Brahman.

nodes.name: Ramana Maharshi
nodes.description: Ramana Maharshi (1879–1950) is one of the most influential Hindu spiritual figures of the 20th century and a towering representative of the Advaita Vedanta tradition — remarkable for the combination of intellectual simplicity, radical directness, and profound inner authority of his teaching. Born Venkataraman Iyer in Tiruchuzhi, Tamil Nadu, he underwent a spontaneous and complete spiritual transformation at age sixteen without any teacher, scripture, or formal practice: overcome by what felt like an approaching death, he lay down, held himself completely rigid, imagined himself dead, and discovered with absolute certainty that the \

nodes.name: Swami Vivekananda
nodes.description:
Swami Vivekananda (born Narendranath Datta, 1863–1902) is one of the most transformative figures in the modern history of Hinduism and in the global spread of Indian philosophy — the person who, perhaps more than any other individual, introduced Vedanta and yoga to Western audiences and gave Hinduism a modern, systematic, and universalistic face. Born in Calcutta to a prosperous and progressive family, he encountered the mystic Ramakrishna Paramahamsa when he was 17, an encounter that transformed his life: Ramakrishna, who had direct mystical experience of the unity underlying all religions, transmitted to Vivekananda a living realization of the non-dual truth and trained him as his primary disciple. After Ramakrishna\

Bringt Vedanta auf das Weltparlament der Religionen und oeffnet ihn fuer den globalen Dialog.

nodes.name: Sri Aurobindo
nodes.description:
Sri Aurobindo (1872–1950) is one of the most original thinkers in modern Indian philosophy — a revolutionary nationalist turned spiritual visionary who developed Integral Yoga as a path for the evolution of consciousness in matter. Educated entirely in England from age seven, he returned to India in 1893 deeply formed by Western thought (he knew Greek, Latin, French, German, Italian, and Spanish fluently) yet without connection to his Hindu heritage, which he subsequently recovered with extraordinary depth. As a leader of the Bengali Extremist faction of the Indian National Congress, he was the most radical voice for complete Indian independence before Gandhi, advocating mass civil disobedience and passive resistance. His arrest in 1908 on sedition charges and his year of solitary confinement in Alipore Jail became his spiritual turning point; he practiced yoga intensively in prison and experienced a series of profound realizations, including a sustained vision of the cosmic consciousness that he later called the Overmind.

Aurobindo withdrew entirely from politics in 1910, moving to Pondicherry (then French territory) where he spent the remaining forty years of his life in intensive spiritual practice, writing, and the development of Integral Yoga. His vision — most fully expressed in The Life Divine — is evolutionary: consciousness is not a late product of matter but the very ground from which matter descended, and evolution is the process by which Spirit, having involved itself in matter, progressively recovers itself through increasingly complex forms — life, mind, and beyond mind, the Supermind. Human beings stand at the cusp of a new evolutionary emergence; the purpose of Integral Yoga is to accelerate this process in the individual and thereby contribute to the transformation of the collective. This is not merely personal liberation (moksha) but the transformation of the Earth.

The Sri Aurobindo Ashram, co-led by Aurobindo and the French-born Mirra Alfassa (known as \

nodes.name: Ibn Arabi
nodes.description:
Muhyiddin Ibn Arabi (1165–1240) is the most systematically profound and metaphysically ambitious mystic in Islamic history, honored as al-Shaykh al-Akbar (the Greatest Master) by the Sufi tradition. Born in Murcia, Andalusia, he traveled extensively across the Islamic world — North Africa, the Middle East, Anatolia — and settled in Damascus, where he died. His output is staggering: over 350 works, of which the two greatest are the Futuhat al-Makkiyya (The Meccan Openings), a 37-volume encyclopedia of mystical knowledge received during his circumambulation of the Kaaba, and the Fusus al-Hikam (Bezels of Wisdom), a compact masterpiece presenting the unique spiritual station of each of twenty-seven prophets from Adam to Muhammad as the facet (bezel) of a divine name.

The central concept of Ibn Arabi\

nodes.name: Karma und Samsara
nodes.description: Samsara (Sanskrit/Pali: \

nodes.name: Jalal ad-Din Rumi
nodes.description: Jalal al-Din Muhammad Rumi (1207–1273) is the most widely read poet in the world today and the supreme literary figure of the Sufi tradition. Born in Balkh (in present-day Afghanistan), he fled westward with his family to escape the Mongol invasions, eventually settling in Konya (in present-day Turkey), where he spent most of his adult life. His early education was in Islamic jurisprudence and theology, but his encounter with the wandering mystic Shams-i-Tabrizi in 1244 transformed him absolutely. The two formed an extraordinary spiritual friendship that lasted three years before Shams mysteriously disappeared (perhaps murdered by jealous disciples). Rumi\

Born in Kanazawa, Japan, he studied Zen under Shaku Soyen and Imagita Kosen, achieving kensho (the initial awakening experience) through intensive koan practice. He accompanied Shaku Soyen to the 1893 Parliament of World\

Ram Dass and Alan Watts are the two most culturally influential bridges between Eastern spiritual traditions and Western seekers in the 20th century. Both began as Western intellectuals (Watts as theologian, Alpert as psychologist), both encountered transformative Eastern teachers or practices, and both spoke from experience rather than mere scholarly knowledge.
